Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Frell (Farscape); Smeg (Red Dwarf); Kriff (Star Wars); Feth (Warhammer 40K)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Original: S1E1 'Saga of a Star World' (1978); Reboot: Miniseries (2003)
Short Description: The most iconic fictional swear word in sci-fi. A universal substitute for the F-word, used in every possible conjugation and context.
Etymology (Narrative): Created by Glen Larson for the 1978 series as 'frack' to bypass ABC network censors. Ronald D. Moore respelled it 'frak' for the 2003 reboot — four letters, matching the word it replaces.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable meaning — always a direct F-word substitute. Spelling changed from 'frack' (1978) to 'frak' (2003), and frequency of use increased dramatically, but the word's function never shifted.
Usage History (Narrative): Original BSG (1978). Reboot BSG (2003-2009). Referenced in Big Bang Theory, Veronica Mars, 30 Rock, Chuck, Eureka, The Flash, and others as homage.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Began as a network-TV workaround. The 2003 reboot used it so pervasively that scholar Wanda Raiford called it essential to the show's naturalistic tone. Crossed into real-world geek vocabulary as a socially acceptable substitute expletive.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across the Twelve Colonies. No regional variation noted within the BSG universe.
Example Quote: What the frak is going on here?

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Mutie (X-Men); Skinjob (Blade Runner); Prawn (District 9); Mudblood (Harry Potter)
Short Description: A racial slur used by humans to dehumanize Cylons, reducing sentient beings to household appliances.
Etymology (Narrative): Derived from the fact that Cylons are machines. Reduces sentient beings to kitchen appliances. The compound 'toaster lover' parallels real-world slurs for those in interracial relationships.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Grew more loaded as humanoid Cylons were revealed — calling someone who looks human a 'toaster' became increasingly cruel and absurd simultaneously.
Usage History (Narrative): BSG reboot (2003-2009). Not present in the 1978 original, which predated this particular human-Cylon dynamic.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Scholar Wanda Raiford drew direct parallels between 'toaster' and real-world dehumanizing slurs, noting the show used it to explore how language enables prejudice. By series end, characters who still used it were implicitly criticized.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used across all Twelve Colonies. The compound 'frakking toasters' combines both key BSG expletives.
Example Quote: Frakking toasters. Can't trust a single one of them.

Short Description: An expletive referring to putrid, crusty grime that accumulates on machinery. Used as an equivalent to bullshit or crap.
Etymology (Narrative): Reportedly derived from a 'felger,' a bovine-like animal with six legs written into early BSG scripts. 'Felgercarb' is presumably its excrement.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable. Always meant 'bullshit/crap.' Fell out of use rather than changing meaning.
Usage History (Narrative): Original BSG (1978). Barely survived into the 2004 reboot, where 'frak' dominated.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Considered too clunky by the reboot's writers. Now remembered as a nostalgic oddity from 1970s sci-fi TV.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across the Twelve Colonies in the original series.
Example Quote: Will you cut the felgercarb and give me a hand?

Short Description: In the Good Place, a cosmic censorship filter automatically replaces all profanity. Eleanor's attempts to curse come out as 'fork,' 'shirt,' 'bench,' and 'ash-hole.'
Etymology (Narrative): Not invented language — an in-universe automatic speech filter in the Good Place neighborhood rewrites profanity in real time, replacing it with near-homophones.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable — the substitution system is consistent throughout all four seasons. The humor comes from Eleanor's frustration, not from the word evolving.
Usage History (Narrative): The Good Place (2016-2020), all four seasons. Critics compared the system to iPhone autocorrect.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Fans adopted 'fork,' 'shirt,' and 'bullshirt' as genuine real-world euphemisms, giving the show's comedy device actual social currency.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Only operative within the Good Place neighborhood. Once characters leave, normal language resumes — a plot point used for dramatic reveals.
Example Quote: Holy forking shirtballs!

Short Description: The Good Place's automatic profanity filter replaces 'shit' with 'shirt.' Also appears as 'bullshirt.'
Etymology (Narrative): Part of the Good Place's cosmic speech filter. Near-homophone of 'shit' selected automatically by the neighborhood's design.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable — no meaning shift.
Usage History (Narrative): The Good Place (2016-2020), all four seasons. Also appears as 'bullshirt.'
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): 'Holy forking shirtballs' became the show's most quotable line and entered casual use among fans.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Only within the Good Place neighborhood.
Example Quote: Oh, this is bullshirt.

Short Description: The Good Place filter's replacement for 'bitch.' Used as both noun and adjective ('son of a bench').
Etymology (Narrative): The Good Place speech filter's replacement for 'bitch.' Near-homophone selected by the neighborhood's cosmic censorship system.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable — no meaning shift.
Usage History (Narrative): The Good Place (2016-2020), all four seasons.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): 'Son of a bench' became a frequently quoted line. Like 'fork' and 'shirt,' it gained mild real-world use among fans.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Only within the Good Place neighborhood.
Example Quote: You know what, you're being a real bench right now.

Short Description: The most versatile curse word in the Red Dwarf universe. Can be used as a noun (smeg), adjective (smegging), or combined with other words (smeghead).
Etymology (Narrative): Created by Rob Grant and Doug Naylor, who insist they did not derive it from 'smegma.' They claim they simply wanted a four-letter word that sounded right. The resemblance is widely noted regardless.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable in meaning but expanded in form. 'Smeghead' emerged as the show's signature insult. Other compounds ('smegging,' 'smeg-for-brains') accumulated over 12 series.
Usage History (Narrative): Red Dwarf Series I-XIII (1988-present). Entered British slang dictionaries.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Its resemblance to 'smegma' gave it authentic vulgarity that purely invented words often lack. Became recognized British English slang beyond the show's fanbase.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used primarily by Lister and Rimmer aboard Red Dwarf. No regional variation — it's a future-English word.
Example Quote: Oh, smeg. What the smegging smeg's he smegging done?

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Frak (BSG); Smeg (Red Dwarf); Fork (Good Place); Kriff (Star Wars)
Short Description: The primary all-purpose expletive in Farscape. Can be used in every context from 'frell off' to 'frelling idiot.' Created by the Sebacean species.
Etymology (Narrative): In-universe, a Sebacean word. Out-of-universe, created by Farscape's writers to allow free cursing on the Sci-Fi Channel.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable — always a direct F-word/damn/hell substitute. Human character Crichton adopting it showed his cultural assimilation into the Uncharted Territories.
Usage History (Narrative): Farscape (1999-2003). The Peacekeeper Wars miniseries (2004). Referenced in Stargate SG-1's Farscape parody episode (S10E6, '200').
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Many fans consider it a better fictional swear than 'frak' due to its sharper sound. Never achieved the same mainstream crossover, partly because Farscape had a smaller audience.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Sebacean in origin, but used across multiple species in the Uncharted Territories. Crichton as a human adopts it naturally.
Example Quote: What the frell is going on?

Short Description: Farscape's equivalent of excrement, used both literally and as a general-purpose expression of disgust or frustration.
Etymology (Narrative): An alien word from the Uncharted Territories. No specific species origin given. Adopted by all crew members aboard Moya.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage throughout the series. Sometimes combined as 'pile of dren' or used in the memorable phrase 'Same dren, different planet.'
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout Farscape's four-season run (1999-2003) and the Peacekeeper Wars miniseries (2004).
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Functions as broadcast-safe scatological profanity. Less iconic than 'frell' but important to Farscape's complete profanity system.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used across multiple species in the Uncharted Territories.
Example Quote: Same dren, different planet.

Short Description: Farscape's equivalent of Hell, used in exclamations like 'What the hezmana?' and 'Go to hezmana.'
Etymology (Narrative): An alien place-name or concept from the Uncharted Territories, analogous to a hellish afterlife or place of punishment.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage throughout the series.
Usage History (Narrative): Used across all four seasons of Farscape. Part of the show's rich alien vocabulary system.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Serves the same cultural function as invoking Hell — expressing shock, anger, or condemning someone to a terrible place.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used across multiple species in the Uncharted Territories.
Example Quote: What the hezmana is wrong with you?

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Shebs (Star Wars Mandalorian); Kelek's balls (Stormlight Archive)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): S1E5 'Back and Back and Back to the Future' (1999)
Short Description: Anatomical slang for testicles in Farscape. Used both literally and in figurative expressions about manipulation.
Etymology (Narrative): Alien anatomical term from the Uncharted Territories. Used to describe being led around or controlled by desire.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent throughout the series. Usually used in the context of someone being manipulated romantically.
Usage History (Narrative): One of Farscape's more colorful anatomical terms, memorable for Claudia Black's delivery.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A mid-level vulgarism — crude but not the most extreme term in Farscape's vocabulary.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used across multiple species.
Example Quote: She's clearly leading D'Argo around by his mivonks.

Short Description: Firefly's frontier-flavored contraction of 'goddamn,' fitting the show's space-western aesthetic perfectly.
Etymology (Narrative): A phonetic contraction of 'God damn' filtered through Firefly's frontier dialect. Fits the show's space-Western speech patterns.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable — no meaning shift. The show's single-season run gave no time for evolution.
Usage History (Narrative): Firefly (2002-2003, 14 episodes). Serenity (2005 film).
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Achieved cultural penetration wildly disproportionate to the show's brief run. Fans regularly use it in real conversation — one of the most adopted fictional swear words in English.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Associated with the outer planets and frontier culture. Inner-world ('Core') characters would be less likely to use it.
Example Quote: Gorramit, Mal, we're humped!

Short Description: Firefly slang for 'cool' or 'great.' Not a curse per se but part of the show's distinctive vernacular that replaced standard exclamations.
Etymology (Narrative): Derived from the idea that in the resource-scarce frontier of the outer planets, something shiny and new is inherently desirable and exciting.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent through the series. Primarily associated with Kaylee's optimistic personality.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout Firefly and adopted enthusiastically by the fan community. 'Shiny' became a hallmark of Browncoat culture.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Never truly taboo — it's the positive end of Firefly's slang spectrum. Its persistence in fan culture shows how deeply the show's language was embraced.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Frontier/outer planet slang. Kaylee uses it most frequently.
Example Quote: Shiny! Let's be bad guys.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Gorram (Firefly); Karabast (Star Wars Rebels); Storms (Stormlight Archive)
Short Description: An expression of frustration or astonishment in the Star Wars galaxy, equivalent to 'goddammit' or 'son of a bitch.' Popularized by The Mandalorian.
Etymology (Narrative): Origin unknown within Star Wars lore. Emerged in The Mandalorian as part of the show's effort to give frontier characters distinctive speech without using Earth profanity.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable — functions as a frustration exclamation. Has not yet developed adjectival forms.
Usage History (Narrative): The Mandalorian S1 (2019). Referenced in memes and merchandise.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A Disney+ creation, so inherently PG-friendly. Despite this, it carries enough emotional weight through performance to feel like genuine swearing.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used across multiple Star Wars worlds. Not tied to a specific species or culture.
Example Quote: Dank farrik, that was close!

Short Description: A Lasat exclamation expressing extreme irritation or surprise. Zeb's signature curse word in Star Wars Rebels.
Etymology (Narrative): A Lasat-language exclamation. Show creator Dave Filoni and writer Greg Weisman confirmed it as an in-universe curse word. Later appeared briefly in Rogue One.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Primarily Zeb's catchphrase throughout Rebels. Made a brief film appearance in Rogue One when Commander Pao uses it during the Battle of Scarif.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout Star Wars Rebels (2014-2018). Briefly crossed into film via Rogue One (2016). Also appeared in the novel Master & Apprentice.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A kid-friendly curse for a Disney XD animated series. Despite its mild deployment, it carries genuine emotional weight through Steve Blum's vocal performance.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Originally Lasat, but adopted by other Rebel soldiers who work alongside Lasat warriors.
Example Quote: Karabast! They've cut off our escape route!

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Hand of Thrawn duology by Timothy Zahn (1997-1998)
Short Description: The Star Wars galaxy's most widespread F-word equivalent. Can be used as verb, adjective (kriffing), or interjection.
Etymology (Narrative): First appeared in Timothy Zahn's Hand of Thrawn duology. Likely derived from a rearrangement of the letters in 'fricking,' a real-world euphemism. No specific in-universe language origin established.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Started in Legends novels. Crossed into Disney canon through the Aftermath trilogy and High Republic comics. Now the standard Star Wars F-word substitute.
Usage History (Narrative): Originated in 1997 Legends novels. Survived the Legends/Canon split and appears in current Disney-era books and comics. One of the few Legends-era profanities to make the transition fully.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Started as a novel-only word. Gradually became the accepted Star Wars equivalent of a strong curse. Its persistence across the Legends-Canon divide shows how essential it became to Star Wars fiction writers.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Widespread across the galaxy. Used by Imperials, Rebels, smugglers, and civilians alike. No species-specific origin.
Example Quote: Schutta? Well, that's just charming. If there's one thing I can't stand, it's bad kriffing language.

Short Description: A Huttese expletive that functions as a strong F-word equivalent. Most prominent in the Legacy-era comics.
Etymology (Narrative): Originated in Huttese, the language of the Hutts. Appears frequently in Star Wars: Legacy comics.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Primarily a Legacy-era term. Less common in other Star Wars eras, where kriff dominates.
Usage History (Narrative): Most prominent in the Star Wars: Legacy comic series (2006-2010). Less widespread than kriff in the broader canon.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A strong expletive that operates at the same taboo level as kriff but with a Huttese cultural origin that gives it a grittier, underworld feel.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Huttese in origin. Associated with the criminal underworld and Outer Rim territories.
Example Quote: Kark on you, you sleemo!

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Tales from Jabba's Palace anthology (1996); Star Wars: Republic Commando (2005)
Short Description: A Huttese curse meaning something vile. Popular among clone commandos in the Republic Commando media.
Etymology (Narrative): Originally Huttese, meaning 'poison' in-universe. Adopted by clone troopers and other military personnel as a general expletive, losing its literal meaning.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Began as a Huttese word meaning 'poison.' Evolved into a general-purpose military expletive among clone troopers, losing its original denotation.
Usage History (Narrative): First appeared in the 1996 Tales from Jabba's Palace anthology. Gained prominence through the 2005 video game Republic Commando and Karen Traviss's novels.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Started as an alien word with a specific meaning. Military adoption stripped it of its literal sense and elevated it to a pure expletive.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Huttese origin but widely adopted by Republic military personnel, especially clone commandos.
Example Quote: Fierfek! We've lost the perimeter!

Franchise: Star Wars (Splinter of the Mind's Eye)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Splinter of the Mind's Eye by Alan Dean Foster (1978)
Short Description: An Alderaanian expletive. One of the oldest invented swear words in the Star Wars expanded universe.
Etymology (Narrative): Introduced as an Alderaanian curse in the very first Star Wars expanded universe novel. One of the earliest attempts to create Star Wars-specific profanity.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained a mild expletive throughout its usage. Never evolved beyond its original function.
Usage History (Narrative): First appeared in 1978, making it one of the oldest Star Wars profanities. Used subsequently in novels, comic books, and games across decades of Star Wars publishing.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Has remained at a consistent mild-to-moderate level throughout its history. Never became as prominent as kriff or even karabast.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Alderaanian in origin. After Alderaan's destruction, the word presumably survived among the diaspora.
Example Quote: Stang! We've been discovered!

Short Description: A common curse in all eras of the Star Wars universe, expressing surprise, anger, or disgust. References the Sith as a source of cosmic evil.
Etymology (Narrative): Combines 'Sith' (the dark side practitioners) with 'spit' to create a blasphemous compound. Originated on Corellia according to some sources.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage as a general expletive. The related 'Sithspawn' refers more specifically to creatures mutated by Sith magic.
Usage History (Narrative): Used across decades of Star Wars publishing. One of the more naturalistic-sounding Star Wars curses.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Derives its taboo from invoking the Sith, the galaxy's most feared practitioners of evil. More blasphemous than scatological.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Corellian in origin according to some sources, but used galaxy-wide.
Example Quote: Oh, Sithspit, we just lost Venom.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Sithspit (Star Wars); Son of a mynock (Star Wars)
Short Description: Both a general curse and a term for creatures genetically engineered by the Sith. As an expletive, it implies something born of pure evil.
Etymology (Narrative): Combines 'Sith' with 'spawn' — offspring. Refers both literally to Sith-created creatures and figuratively as an insult or exclamation.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Dual meaning — started as a creature classification and evolved into a general expletive. Both uses persist.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Dark Empire II (1994-1995). Used in both Legends and Canon materials.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): The dual literal/figurative meaning gives it extra weight. Calling someone a Sithspawn is both blasphemous and implies monstrous origin.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Corellian in origin but used galaxy-wide. Both the creatures and the curse originate from Sith-influenced regions.
Example Quote: That Sithspawn nearly took my arm off!

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Frell off (Farscape); Frak off (BSG); Zark off (Hitchhiker's)
Real-World Euphemisms: Screw you; Get lost; Buzz off
Short Description: A Huttese curse uttered by a silver protocol droid to C-3PO in Cloud City. C-3PO's shocked 'How rude!' suggests it's deeply offensive.
Etymology (Narrative): Huttese phrase. Its exact translation is deliberately left ambiguous by Lucasfilm, but C-3PO's scandalized reaction implies it's quite vulgar.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained a mysterious, untranslated insult. Its power comes from its ambiguity.
Usage History (Narrative): Appeared in The Empire Strikes Back (1980). One of the earliest in-universe curse words in Star Wars film canon. Referenced and discussed by fans for decades.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Its deliberate lack of translation makes it more intriguing. The audience infers its severity entirely from C-3PO's reaction, which is a masterclass in implied profanity.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Huttese. Used in Cloud City, Bespin.
Example Quote: E chu ta! — How rude!

Short Description: A Huttese insult translating roughly to 'slimeball.' Used by young Anakin on Tatooine.
Etymology (Narrative): Huttese word. Anakin, growing up as a slave on Tatooine among Hutts, naturally uses Huttese insults.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained a mild insult throughout its usage. Never evolved beyond its original meaning.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in The Phantom Menace (1999) and subsequent Star Wars media.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A mild insult suitable for a PG-rated film featuring a child protagonist.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Huttese. Common on Hutt-controlled worlds like Tatooine.
Example Quote: Cho skrunee dopat, sleemo! (Don't count on it, slimeball!)

Short Description: An alien word literally meaning excrement. Used in compounds like 'a druk-load of problems.'
Etymology (Narrative): An alien loan-word that entered Galactic Basic as a scatological term. Its literal meaning is excrement.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained consistently scatological. No semantic drift.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in various Star Wars novels as a stronger alternative to 'bantha fodder.'
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderately strong scatological term. More vulgar than 'bantha poodoo' but less versatile than kriff.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Outer Rim origin. Common among military personnel and smugglers.
Example Quote: We've got a druk-load of problems heading our way.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Dren (Farscape); Druk (Star Wars); Osik (Star Wars Mandalorian)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Republic Commando: Triple Zero by Karen Traviss (2006)
Short Description: A euphemism for excrement used by clone troopers during the Clone Wars.
Etymology (Narrative): Part of the military slang adopted by clone troopers. Karen Traviss created extensive Mandalorian and clone military vocabulary.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained military slang. Did not spread beyond the clone trooper/Mandalorian cultural sphere.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Karen Traviss's Republic Commando novels (2006+). Part of a rich military vocabulary system.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderately vulgar military term. Part of the distinctive clone trooper speech patterns.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by clone troopers trained on Kamino. Likely Mandalorian-influenced given Jango Fett's heritage.
Example Quote: Step in dwang and you'll bring the stink with you.

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Republic Commando: Hard Contact by Karen Traviss (2004)
Short Description: A Mandalorian swear word meaning buttocks. Used in insults and expressions of contempt.
Etymology (Narrative): Mando'a (Mandalorian language) term created by Karen Traviss as part of her extensive Mandalorian cultural vocabulary.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained anatomical slang within the Mandalorian cultural sphere.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Karen Traviss's Republic Commando series. Part of the broader Mando'a language she developed.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderate vulgarity. Anatomical but not the strongest term in the Mandalorian vocabulary.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Mandalorian-specific. Used on Mandalore and by Mandalorian diaspora cultures.
Example Quote: Get off your shebs and fight!

Short Description: An Orkan curse word used by Mork, an alien from the planet Ork. Briefly became a real-world pop culture catchphrase in the late 1970s-80s.
Etymology (Narrative): Reportedly improvised by Robin Williams on set. Thought to be a blend of 'Shazam' (the DC superhero exclamation) and 'robot.' No official etymology exists.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained Mork's signature exclamation throughout the series. Did not develop additional forms or conjugations.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout Mork & Mindy's four seasons (1978-1982). Crossed into mainstream pop culture as a catchphrase. For about a decade, 'shazbot' was a widely recognized pseudo-curse.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Peaked in the late 1970s-early 1980s as a genuine pop culture phenomenon. Faded with the show's end but retained nostalgic recognition. Often cited as the proto-example of fictional TV profanity.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Orkan. Used exclusively by Mork, the only Orkan character seen regularly.
Example Quote: Shazbot! I can't believe I did that!

English Equivalent: Fuck (most offensive word in the galaxy)
Franchise: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
Real-World Euphemisms: The F-word; The worst word imaginable
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Life, the Universe and Everything by Douglas Adams (1982; originally in The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy radio series)
Short Description: In the Hitchhiker's universe, 'Belgium' is the most unspeakably offensive word in the galaxy. It was introduced when American publishers refused to print the original F-word.
Etymology (Narrative): Douglas Adams originally wrote an actual English profanity. When American publishers refused to print it, Adams replaced it with 'Belgium' and added a passage explaining it was the most profane word in the galaxy — turning censorship into comedy.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Started as a censorship workaround. Became canonical in the Hitchhiker's universe. The joke is that something utterly mundane on Earth is cosmically offensive everywhere else.
Usage History (Narrative): Originated in the radio series, formalized in Life, the Universe and Everything (1982). The 2005 film included the concept. Remains one of the most celebrated examples of meta-fictional profanity.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Unique trajectory — began as a real-world censorship dodge that Adams turned into a joke about the arbitrariness of taboo language. It's simultaneously the mildest and most extreme fictional curse word.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Offensive throughout the galaxy EXCEPT on Earth, where it's merely a small country. This disconnect is the joke.
Example Quote: Belgium, man, Belgium!

Franchise: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Life, the Universe and Everything by Douglas Adams (1982)
Short Description: A verb used like a substitute for 'fucking.' Likely derived from the prophet Zarquon in-universe.
Etymology (Narrative): Probably derived from Zarquon, a messianic figure in the Hitchhiker's universe. Using a holy figure's name as profanity follows real-world blasphemy patterns.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage as an expletive modifier throughout the Hitchhiker's series.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in the later Hitchhiker's books. Less famous than 'Belgium' but part of the same profanity system.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Functions as standard blasphemous profanity within the Hitchhiker's universe.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Galaxy-wide usage in the Hitchhiker's universe.
Example Quote: Zark off.

Real-World Euphemisms: Racial slur; Ethnic slur; Blood-based bigotry term
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets (1998 book / 2002 film)
Short Description: A deeply offensive slur used by blood-purist wizards against Muggle-born witches and wizards. Functions as the wizarding world's equivalent of the most extreme racial slurs.
Etymology (Narrative): Compound of 'mud' (impure) and 'blood' (lineage). Implies Muggle-born wizards have dirty blood unworthy of magical society. Reflects real-world racial purity ideology.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Escalates across the series. Schoolyard insult in Chamber of Secrets. Institutional weapon in Deathly Hallows, where the Ministry uses 'Mudblood Registration' as a tool of persecution.
Usage History (Narrative): Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets (1998 book, 2002 film). Central to all subsequent books and films through Deathly Hallows.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): One of the most analyzed fictional slurs in literary criticism. Its escalation from personal bigotry to state-sponsored persecution parallels historical registries targeting ethnic minorities.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used primarily by old pure-blood families (Malfoys, Blacks, Lestranges). Considered unspeakable by most of wizarding society. More common in Britain's wizarding world where blood-purist ideology is strongest.
Example Quote: No one asked your opinion, you filthy little Mudblood!

Short Description: An insult used by blood-purists against pure-blood wizards who associate with Muggle-borns or advocate for Muggle rights. The Weasley family is frequently targeted with this term.
Etymology (Narrative): Compound insult implying betrayal of one's 'blood' (pure-blood heritage) by fraternizing with those of 'lesser' blood. Mirrors real-world terms like 'race traitor.'
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage throughout the series. Always carries extreme contempt from the speaker.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout the Harry Potter series, primarily by Death Eaters and their sympathizers targeting the Weasley family and Dumbledore's allies.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Carries the full weight of ideological betrayal. In the blood-purist worldview, consorting with Muggle-borns is as offensive as being one.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Pure-blood supremacist circles in the British wizarding world.
Example Quote: The Weasleys are the biggest blood traitors of them all.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Mutie (X-Men); Mud people (Artemis Fowl); Blanker (Noughts and Crosses)
Short Description: A bigoted term used against werewolves, centaurs, merpeople, and half-giants — anyone perceived as not fully human or not fully wizard.
Etymology (Narrative): Direct adaptation of a real-world slur applied to the wizarding context. Targets species mixing rather than ethnic mixing.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used consistently as a term of contempt. Applied to Hagrid (half-giant), Remus Lupin (werewolf), and Firenze (centaur).
Usage History (Narrative): Used by characters like Umbridge and various Death Eaters throughout the series.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Functions identically to its real-world counterpart, carrying implications of impurity and lesser worth.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by human wizarding supremacists in the British wizarding world.
Example Quote: I really hate children. And half-breeds.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Gonk (Cyberpunk); S'wit (Elder Scrolls); Woolhead (Wheel of Time)
Short Description: The signature insult of Red Dwarf. 'Smeghead' combines the show's all-purpose expletive with 'head' to create an insult roughly equivalent to 'dickhead.'
Etymology (Narrative): Compound of 'smeg' (the show's invented expletive) and 'head.' The creators insist 'smeg' was not derived from 'smegma,' though the connection is widely assumed by audiences.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Started as a simple insult. Became the show's single most iconic word, used more than any other profanity. 'Smeghead' transcended the show to become recognized British slang.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout all series of Red Dwarf since 1988. One of the most recognized fictional insults in British comedy. Entered the Oxford English Dictionary as informal British slang.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Started as a broadcast-safe insult for BBC. Gained real cultural currency in Britain. Its resemblance to 'smegma' gave it an authentic vulgarity that purely invented words often lack.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used aboard Red Dwarf and in future Earth settings. No regional variation.
Example Quote: Rimmer, you're such a smeghead.

Short Description: An insult in the Red Dwarf universe meaning a stupid or worthless person.
Etymology (Narrative): An invented future-English insult. No clear etymology within the show's universe.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained a mild insult throughout the series, overshadowed by the more iconic 'smeghead.'
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout Red Dwarf. Less prominent than 'smeg' but part of the show's distinctive vocabulary.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A mild insult. Less taboo than 'smeghead' but still dismissive.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used aboard Red Dwarf.
Example Quote: Listen here, you total gimboid.

Real-World Euphemisms: Damn; Blast; The worst word imaginable
Notable Speaker: Various fairy characters, especially Mulch Diggums
Short Description: A Gnommish swear word described by the author as so severe when translated that it would need to be censored.
Etymology (Narrative): A word from Gnommish, the fairy language in Artemis Fowl. Author Eoin Colfer deliberately left it untranslated, stating only that it was too strong for the page.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained consistently the strongest curse in the fairy vocabulary throughout the series.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout the Artemis Fowl series (2001-2012). Became a favorite real-world substitute expletive among the book's young readers.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Positioned as the most extreme fairy expletive. Its untranslated status increases its mystique and perceived severity.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by fairy (Lower Elements) species. Not typically used by humans in the Artemis Fowl world.
Example Quote: D'Arvit! They've breached the lower levels!

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Mud-monkey (Supernatural); Normie (Discworld); Muggle (Harry Potter as condescending)
Short Description: The fairy term for humans, implying they are dirty, surface-dwelling primitives compared to the technologically advanced underground fairy civilization.
Etymology (Narrative): Fairies live underground in high-tech cities. Humans live on the muddy surface. The term reduces humans to their surface habitat, implying primitiveness.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Started as casual prejudice. As the series progresses and fairies work with Artemis, some characters moderate their language while others double down.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout the Artemis Fowl series. Central to the books' exploration of inter-species prejudice.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Begins as universal fairy condescension toward humans. Characters who befriend Artemis gradually stop using it, while antagonists continue.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal among fairy (Lower Elements) society.
Example Quote: The Mud People are destroying the surface. Again.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Smegging (Red Dwarf); Frelling (Farscape); Kriffing (Star Wars); Gorram (Firefly)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): The Way of Kings by Brandon Sanderson (2010)
Short Description: The primary profane modifier in the Stormlight Archive. Derives from the devastating highstorms that dominate life on Roshar.
Etymology (Narrative): On Roshar, highstorms are immensely destructive hurricanes central to all life. Using 'storms' as profanity reflects the deep cultural impact of this ever-present natural force.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable — consistently the primary profane modifier. Works as exclamation ('Storms!'), adjective ('storming'), and verb phrase ('storm it').
Usage History (Narrative): The Way of Kings (2010) through Wind and Truth (2024). All five published Stormlight Archive novels.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Widely cited as an exemplary case of worldbuilding-derived profanity. Feels natural because the thing it invokes is genuinely feared by every character.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across Roshar. Used by Alethi, Veden, and other Rosharan cultures.
Example Quote: Storming lighteyes! You storming fool!

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Dren (Farscape); Druk (Star Wars); Fewmets (Dragonriders of Pern)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): The Way of Kings by Brandon Sanderson (2010)
Short Description: The most common mild swear word on Roshar. Crem is the stony mineral runoff carried by highstorms that hardens and coats everything.
Etymology (Narrative): Crem is a real substance on Roshar — mineral deposits left by highstorm rains. It clogs pipes, covers buildings, and must be constantly chiseled away. It's the perfect mild swear word because everyone deals with it and finds it annoying.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remains mild throughout the series. Functions like 'crap' — vulgar but not shocking.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout the Stormlight Archive. One of the most praised examples of worldbuilding-derived profanity in fantasy literature.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Remains mild. It's the 'crap' of Roshar — something everyone says, even in polite company. Not the strongest oath but universally understood.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across Roshar. Every culture deals with crem buildup after highstorms.
Example Quote: What a load of crem.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Blood and ashes (Wheel of Time); Kelek's breath (Stormlight); By the Throne (Warhammer 40K)
Real-World Euphemisms: God damn; Good Lord; Christ almighty; Holy cow
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): The Alloy of Law by Brandon Sanderson (2011)
Short Description: A blasphemous exclamation invoking Ruin, one of the two primal gods of Scadrial. 'Rust' refers to the destructive force of entropy.
Etymology (Narrative): In Scadrial's theology, Ruin and Preservation are the two primal forces. 'Rust' is associated with Ruin's entropy. Invoking them is blasphemous because these are real, documented divine forces in this world.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent through Mistborn Era 2. The phrase and its variants (just 'Rust!' or 'Rusts!') are the primary blasphemous exclamations.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in The Alloy of Law (2011). Part of Sanderson's consistent approach of tying profanity to world-specific theology.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Functions as genuine blasphemy in a world where the gods were real, documented entities. Carries more theological weight than most fictional oaths.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used in the Elendel Basin region of Scadrial during Era 2.
Example Quote: Rust and Ruin, that was close!

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Rust and Ruin (Mistborn); Holy forking shirtballs (Good Place); By the Light (WoT)
Real-World Euphemisms: God damn it; Son of a gun; Bloody hell
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): The Eye of the World by Robert Jordan (1990)
Short Description: The Wheel of Time's most iconic oath. A compound profanity expressing extreme frustration, surprise, or anger.
Etymology (Narrative): 'Ashes' likely reference destruction and death. 'Blood' adds visceral intensity. A culturally specific compound oath. Robert Jordan deliberately avoided real-world profanity.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable throughout all 14 books. Mat Cauthon uses it most, making it almost his personal catchphrase.
Usage History (Narrative): The Wheel of Time books (1990-2013, 14 volumes). Amazon TV adaptation (2021-present).
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Represents the strongest tier of the WoT profanity system, which is notably mild compared to contemporaries like ASOIAF. Roughly equivalent to a vigorous 'goddammit.'
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used across the Westlands. Mat Cauthon, being from the Two Rivers, uses it most, but it's widespread.
Example Quote: Blood and bloody ashes! What's going on?

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Storms (Stormlight); By the Maker (Dragon Age); Kelek's breath (Stormlight)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): The Eye of the World by Robert Jordan (1990)
Short Description: An exclamation invoking the Light, the divine force of good in the Wheel of Time cosmology. Used as one would say 'God!' or 'Good Lord!'
Etymology (Narrative): The Light is the fundamental force of good in WoT cosmology, opposed by the Dark One. Invoking it in exclamation follows the pattern of using divine names as mild profanity.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent throughout the series. Used in many forms: 'Light!', 'Light help me', 'Thank the Light', 'By the Light'.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout all 14 Wheel of Time books and the TV adaptation.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Mild blasphemy. Everyone in the Westlands invokes the Light casually. It's the baseline level of profanity.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across the Westlands.
Example Quote: Light! I thought you were dead!

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): The Eye of the World by Robert Jordan (1990)
Short Description: An insult meaning someone stubbornly denying truth or acting foolishly. Nynaeve's signature put-down.
Etymology (Narrative): Derived from 'wool' (sheep-like, not thinking clearly) and 'headed.' Suggests someone with wool for brains who refuses to see reason.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained Nynaeve's signature insult throughout the entire series.
Usage History (Narrative): Used extensively throughout all 14 Wheel of Time books, most frequently by Nynaeve al'Meara.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Mild by any standard. Part of the Wheel of Time's generally restrained approach to profanity. Its charm is in its folksy specificity.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Two Rivers / Westlands expression.
Example Quote: Men! Wool-headed fools, the lot of them!

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Son of a mynock (Star Wars); Sithspawn (Star Wars)
Real-World Euphemisms: Son of a bitch; Motherfucker; Bloody hell
Short Description: An oath expressing extreme disgust or shock. Its vulgarity is in the implication rather than explicit meaning.
Etymology (Narrative): The expression implies something so shocking it makes one revert to infantile needs. The vulgarity is in the imagery of regression and degradation.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used as one of the stronger exclamations in the series. Reserved for moments of genuine shock or outrage.
Usage History (Narrative): Scattered throughout the Wheel of Time series. Less frequent than 'blood and ashes' but stronger.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): One of the strongest oaths in the Wheel of Time's deliberately PG-13 profanity system.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used across the Westlands, often by soldiers and rougher characters.
Example Quote: Mother's milk in a cup! That's the Dragon Reborn!

Short Description: A Dunmeri slur meaning 'outlander' or 'foreigner.' Used by Dark Elves against anyone not native to Morrowind, carrying intense xenophobic contempt.
Etymology (Narrative): Dunmeris (Dark Elf language). Literally means 'outlander' but carries connotations comparable to xenophobic slurs like 'gaijin' or 'gweilo' at their most contemptuous.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Gains bitter irony after the Red Year volcanic eruption. Dunmer who once used it become refugees themselves, experiencing the xenophobia they directed at others.
Usage History (Narrative): Morrowind (2002). Referenced in Oblivion (2006), Skyrim (2011), and Elder Scrolls Online (2014+).
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Became iconic through countless player encounters with hostile Morrowind NPCs. The post-Red-Year irony adds narrative depth across the franchise's timeline.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Specifically Dunmeri (Dark Elf). Used on Morrowind/Vvardenfell. Less common among Dunmer diaspora in Skyrim.
Example Quote: You N'wah! What do you want?

Short Description: A Dunmeri insult roughly equivalent to calling someone a fool or an idiot, with the vulgarity of calling them 'shit.'
Etymology (Narrative): From Dunmeris. The exact meaning is debated — some sources suggest 'fool' while others suggest it's closer to scatological profanity.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage across Elder Scrolls games featuring Dunmer characters.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Morrowind (2002). Used by Dunmer NPCs in subsequent games.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderate Dunmeri insult. Stronger than casual name-calling but less charged than the slur N'wah.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Specifically Dunmeri. Used primarily on Morrowind/Vvardenfell and by Dunmer diaspora.
Example Quote: You s'wit! How are we supposed to open this chest without the lockpicks!

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Smeghead (Red Dwarf); Slitch (various); Scruffy nerf-herder (Star Wars)
Short Description: A Dunmeri insult used to describe someone contemptible. The exact denotation is unclear but carries strong negative connotations.
Etymology (Narrative): From Dunmeris. May relate to fetching (as in a servant or lackey), implying subservience. Others interpret it as closer to a vulgar anatomical insult.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent throughout the Elder Scrolls series whenever Dunmer characters appear.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Morrowind (2002). Used in Oblivion, Skyrim, and Elder Scrolls Online.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A versatile Dunmeri insult, roughly at the 'bastard' level of intensity.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Dunmeri. Used across all Dunmer communities.
Example Quote: Die, fetcher!

Short Description: A Nordic insult implying someone is so infantile they were never weaned off their mother's breast milk. Implies extreme weakness.
Etymology (Narrative): In Nordic warrior culture, drinking mead and ale signifies adulthood and strength. Calling someone a 'milk-drinker' implies they're still an infant, too weak for alcohol and combat.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used consistently in Skyrim. Became a major meme in gaming culture, beloved for its absurd specificity.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Skyrim (2011). Became one of the most quoted and memed lines in gaming. Frequently referenced in other media and internet culture.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Mild within the game but culturally devastating in-universe. In a warrior culture that values strength and drinking, it questions your fundamental adulthood.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Specifically Nordic/Skyrim. Used by Nords throughout the province.
Example Quote: What's the matter? Can't stand the sight of a strong Nord woman? You're nothing but a milk-drinker.

Real-World Euphemisms: Oh my God; Good grief; Crikey; Blimey
Short Description: A Dunmeri exclamation of surprise, roughly equivalent to 'oh my God' or 'damn.'
Etymology (Narrative): A Dunmeris exclamation. May be related to one of the Tribunal deities or Daedric Princes invoked in shock.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent as a mild exclamation. No evolution beyond its original function.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in Elder Scrolls Online and referenced in lore texts.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Mild blasphemous exclamation. The Dunmeri equivalent of casually invoking a deity's name.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Dunmeri. Used primarily by Dark Elves.
Example Quote: B'vek! What happened here?

Short Description: An Argonian (Jel language) exclamation used to express frustration or anger.
Etymology (Narrative): From Jel, the Argonian language. Part of the Elder Scrolls' system of giving each race its own profanity reflecting their distinct culture.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used by Argonian characters. Has not developed beyond its basic exclamatory function.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in Elder Scrolls Online and later Elder Scrolls media featuring Argonian characters.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderate Argonian expletive. Less iconic than the Dunmeri curses due to Argonians' less prominent role in main-series games.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Argonian (Black Marsh). Used by Argonians regardless of location.
Example Quote: Xuth! We're surrounded!

Short Description: Argonian (Jel) word for crap or excrement, used as a scatological expletive.
Etymology (Narrative): From Jel, the Argonian language. Scatological profanity following the universal pattern of using waste as an insult.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent scatological term for Argonian characters.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in Elder Scrolls Online.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Standard scatological vulgarity in the Argonian lexicon.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Argonian (Black Marsh).
Example Quote: What a load of kaoc.

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Cyberpunk TTRPG by Mike Pondsmith (1988); Cyberpunk 2077 (2020)
Short Description: Night City slang for an idiot or fool. Can be used as noun or adjective ('gonk move'). One of Cyberpunk's most beloved and adopted slang terms.
Etymology (Narrative): Created by Mike Pondsmith for the 1988 Cyberpunk tabletop RPG. Onomatopoeic and slightly comical, with no clear real-world etymology.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Stable — always means 'idiot/fool.' Can function as noun ('you gonk') or adjective ('gonk move').
Usage History (Narrative): Cyberpunk TTRPG (1988). Cyberpunk 2077 (2020). Cyberpunk: Edgerunners anime (2022).
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Became one of the most recognizable gaming slang terms of the 2020s after the video game and anime brought it to mainstream audiences.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across Night City. All social strata and gangs use it.
Example Quote: Don't be a gonk, V. Think before you act.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Toaster (BSG); Mutie (X-Men); Mudblood (Harry Potter); Skinjob (Blade Runner)
Notable Speaker: Wikus Van De Merwe (Sharlto Copley), various humans
Short Description: A derogatory term used by humans for the alien Poleepkwa, comparing them to bottom-feeding crustaceans. The film uses it as an apartheid allegory.
Etymology (Narrative): The aliens resemble large prawns (shrimp). Humans seized on their appearance to create a dehumanizing slur, reducing sentient beings to seafood. The film explicitly parallels South African apartheid.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used consistently as a slur throughout the film. No in-film drift, but the audience's understanding of its cruelty deepens as the film reveals the aliens' intelligence and suffering.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in District 9 (2009). The film's apartheid allegory made 'prawn' one of the most analyzed fictional slurs in film criticism.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Immediately extreme within the film's context. The word is loaded with contempt and used to justify segregation, eviction, and violence — directly paralleling real-world dehumanizing language.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by human South Africans. The aliens themselves do not use the term.
Example Quote: A prawn wouldn't know the difference anyway.

Short Description: A derogatory term for replicants — bioengineered humanoids. Implies they're merely skin stretched over machinery, denying their potential personhood.
Etymology (Narrative): Combines 'skin' (their human-like exterior) with 'job' (manufactured product). The term reduces sentient beings to their manufactured components. Adopted into BSG and other sci-fi.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used in both Blade Runner (1982) and Blade Runner 2049 (2017), showing the slur persists across decades of in-universe time.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Blade Runner (1982). Used in the sequel 2049 (2017). Referenced in BSG where 'skin job' was used for human-looking Cylons.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Began as police slang in the original film. By 2049, it's deeply embedded institutional language. The term's persistence shows how slurs calcify over generations.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by human police and civilians in future Los Angeles.
Example Quote: Christ, Deckard, you look almost as bad as that skinjob you left on the sidewalk.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Mudblood (Harry Potter); Prawn (District 9); Toaster (BSG); N'wah (Elder Scrolls)
Notable Speaker: Various anti-mutant characters; Donald Pierce in Logan
Short Description: A slur directed at mutants in the Marvel Universe. Sometimes referred to as 'the m-word.' Became the Marvel equivalent of the most extreme real-world racial slurs.
Etymology (Narrative): Shortened form of 'mutant,' weaponized into a slur. Parallels how neutral descriptors can be twisted into epithets when applied with contempt by a dominant group.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Introduced in early X-Men comics. By the 1980s, explicitly positioned as the Marvel Universe's equivalent of the N-word. Resurfaced prominently in The Gifted TV series.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in X-Men comics since the 1960s. Featured in Logan (2017) and The Gifted (2017-2019). One of the longest-running fictional slurs in popular culture.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Escalated from casual prejudice in early comics to systematic oppression. In storylines like 'Days of Future Past,' it accompanies genocide.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used globally in the Marvel Universe wherever anti-mutant sentiment exists. Especially prominent during Sentinel programs and mutant registration acts.
Example Quote: You're nothing but a filthy mutie.

English Equivalent: Slur for humans who sleep with vampires
Short Description: A slur for humans who have sexual relationships with vampires. Shames them for crossing the human-vampire divide.
Etymology (Narrative): Compound of 'fang' (vampire teeth) and 'banger' (slang for sexual partner). Combines species prejudice with sexual shaming.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used consistently throughout True Blood's seven seasons. Part of the show's extensive prejudice vocabulary.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout True Blood (2008-2014). Part of the show's allegory for various forms of real-world prejudice.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Loaded with both species-ism and sexual shaming. Functions like a compound slur — targeting both the vampire association and the sexual element.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used across the American South in True Blood's setting. Particularly common in anti-vampire communities.
Example Quote: You horny fangbangers are literally asking for it.

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): The Smurfs TV series (1981); The Smurfs film (2011)
Short Description: The Smurfs replace nearly any word with 'smurf,' which when applied to profanity, creates implied swearing that can be shockingly vulgar in implication.
Etymology (Narrative): Created by Belgian cartoonist Peyo (Pierre Culliford) in 1958. The word 'smurf' (French: 'schtroumpf') was reportedly coined at dinner when Peyo forgot the word for salt and said 'pass the schtroumpf.'
Semantic Drift Timeline: In the 1981 TV series, 'smurf' replaced ordinary words. In the 2011 film, the joke was made explicit when a character says 'Smurfity-smurf-smurf-smurf' and is told there's 'no call for that kind of language.'
Usage History (Narrative): Used since the 1958 comics. The TV series (1981-1989) and films (2011, 2013, 2017) all use the substitution system.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Unique trajectory — a children's cartoon word that, when adult audiences think about what's being replaced, becomes implicit profanity. The 2011 film made this explicit for laughs.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal among Smurf Village. Humans find the substitution confusing.
Example Quote: Patrick: Stop saying 'smurf' for everything! Smurf! Smurfity-smurf-smurf-smurf! (All the smurfs gasp.) Gutsy: There's no call for that kind of language, laddie!

Short Description: An insult implying someone is an unsophisticated rural laborer. Leia calls Han Solo a 'stuck-up, half-witted, scruffy-looking nerf herder.'
Etymology (Narrative): Nerfs are large, smelly herd animals in the Star Wars galaxy. Herding them is unglamorous, low-status work. The insult implies someone is crude and beneath notice.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Remained a mild insult. Most famous for Leia's delivery. Has not evolved beyond its original usage.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in The Empire Strikes Back (1980). Became one of Star Wars' most quoted insults. Referenced in countless parodies and homages.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Mild by any standard. It's the Star Wars equivalent of calling someone a farmer or yokel. Its fame comes from Carrie Fisher's delivery, not its severity.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Galaxy-wide term. Nerfs exist on many planets.
Example Quote: Why, you stuck-up, half-witted, scruffy-looking nerf herder!

Notable Speaker: Colonel-Commissar Ibram Gaunt, Tanith First and Only
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): First and Only by Dan Abnett (1999)
Short Description: The primary expletive of the Tanith First and Only regiment. Derived from an ancient tree spirit associated with fertility on their destroyed homeworld.
Etymology (Narrative): Named after an ancient fertility-associated tree spirit from the planet Tanith. The connection between a fertility deity and profanity follows real-world linguistic patterns where sexual/reproductive concepts become taboo.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage throughout the Gaunt's Ghosts series. Remains specifically associated with the Tanith regiment.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Dan Abnett's First and Only (1999). Used throughout the Gaunt's Ghosts series. Part of the rich regimental culture Abnett created.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Functions as a strong military expletive. Its connection to a lost homeworld's deity gives it additional emotional resonance for the Tanith soldiers.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Specific to Tanith and the Tanith First and Only regiment. Other Imperial Guard regiments have their own profanity.
Example Quote: Fething hell, that was close!

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Gaunt's Ghosts series by Dan Abnett
Short Description: Scatological profanity in the Gaunt's Ghosts series, equivalent to 'shit' or 'crap.'
Etymology (Narrative): An Imperial Guard military term. Part of the future military slang system Dan Abnett created for the Warhammer 40K universe.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent scatological term throughout the series.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in the Gaunt's Ghosts novels as part of the Imperial Guard vocabulary.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Standard military scatological vulgarity. Not the strongest term available but widely used.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Imperial Guard / Astra Militarum military slang.
Example Quote: This situation has gone completely to gak.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Nerf herder (Star Wars); Son of a mynock (Star Wars)
Short Description: A grox is a large, aggressive, notoriously ill-tempered beast in the 40K universe. Used as an insult comparing someone to this animal.
Etymology (Narrative): Grox are bad-tempered livestock in the Warhammer 40K universe. Like calling someone a 'pig' or 'swine,' it compares the target to a disagreeable animal.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent animal-based insult throughout 40K literature.
Usage History (Narrative): Used across various Warhammer 40K novels and game materials.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderate insult. Animal comparisons are universal across human cultures.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Galaxy-wide Imperial slang.
Example Quote: Don't be such a grox about it.

Short Description: Standard scatological profanity in the Shadowrun universe. Equivalent to 'shit' or 'crap.'
Etymology (Narrative): Derived from 'dreck' (Yiddish/German for trash or waste). Modified spelling for the cyberpunk future setting.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage from the 1989 TTRPG through video game adaptations.
Usage History (Narrative): Used since the original 1989 Shadowrun TTRPG. Featured in the video game series (2013-2015) and all subsequent media.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A well-established cyberpunk expletive. Its Yiddish roots give it real-world linguistic credibility.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across the Shadowrun Sixth World.
Example Quote: This plan is pure drek, chummer.

Short Description: A strong vulgar term in the Shadowrun universe, used similarly to the F-word. Has a particularly 'greasy' and aggressive connotation.
Etymology (Narrative): Possibly derived from cybernetic 'slots' (ports for installing cyberware). The sexual connotation follows the pattern of mechanical insertion metaphors becoming profanity.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage as a strong expletive throughout Shadowrun media.
Usage History (Narrative): Used since the 1989 TTRPG. Part of Shadowrun's extensive slang vocabulary.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): One of the stronger terms in Shadowrun's vocabulary. More aggressive than 'drek.'
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across the Sixth World.
Example Quote: Slot off, you fragger.

Short Description: The primary F-word substitute in Shadowrun. Used as verb, noun, and adjective just like its real-world counterpart.
Etymology (Narrative): Derived from military slang 'frag' (to kill a fellow soldier, especially an officer, typically with a fragmentation grenade). In Shadowrun's violent future, this military term became general profanity.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage from 1989 to present. One of the most versatile Shadowrun expletives.
Usage History (Narrative): Used since the original 1989 TTRPG. Featured in all Shadowrun media including novels, video games, and sourcebooks.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Started as military jargon, became general profanity. One of the more linguistically realistic fictional curse word evolutions.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal across the Sixth World.
Example Quote: Frag it! We're fragged!

Short Description: A derogatory term for elves in the Shadowrun universe, derived from 'Keebler Elves' (a cookie brand featuring elf mascots).
Etymology (Narrative): From 'Keebler,' a real-world cookie brand whose mascots are cartoon elves. Reducing a proud metahuman species to a cookie mascot is deliberately demeaning.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent slur used against elves throughout Shadowrun media.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in Shadowrun novels and sourcebooks. Part of the system's exploration of metahuman racism in a cyberpunk setting.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Carries the weight of metahuman prejudice. In Shadowrun's world, anti-metahuman bigotry is institutionalized and violent.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by human supremacist groups like Humanis Policlub.
Example Quote: Stinking keebs think they own this neighborhood.

Short Description: A derogatory term for orks and trolls collectively, derived from 'troglodyte' — implying they're primitive cave-dwellers.
Etymology (Narrative): Shortened from 'troglodyte,' meaning cave-dweller. Implies orks and trolls are sub-human primitives despite being fully sapient metahumans.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent slur throughout Shadowrun media.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in Shadowrun since the original TTRPG. Part of the metahuman prejudice system.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Dehumanizing species slur. Part of Shadowrun's ongoing exploration of how bigotry adapts to new targets.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by human supremacists. Orks sometimes reclaim related terms.
Example Quote: No trogs allowed in this establishment.

English Equivalent: Slur for those who fraternize with grounders
Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Fangbanger (True Blood); Toaster lover (BSG); Blood traitor (Harry Potter)
Short Description: An insult used by space-station-born characters against those who form relationships with Earth-dwelling 'Grounders.'
Etymology (Narrative): Compound of 'Grounder' (the term for Earth survivors) and 'pounder' (sexual implication). Combines cultural prejudice with sexual shaming, like 'fangbanger.'
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used in early seasons. As alliances form between Sky People and Grounders, the term loses some currency.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in The 100's first seasons (2014+). Reflects the initial hostility between the two human factions.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Initially very charged. Loses some power as the show's factions intermingle, reflecting how prejudice can decrease with exposure.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by Sky People (Ark-born). Not used by Grounders.
Example Quote: I had no idea you were such a grounder-pounder.

Short Description: An all-purpose swear word from Larry Niven's Known Space universe. Acronym for 'There Ain't No Justice.'
Etymology (Narrative): Acronym: T.A.N.J. — There Ain't No Justice. A nihilistic expression of cosmic unfairness that became a general-purpose expletive. Reflects the series' libertarian-influenced worldview.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage throughout the Known Space/Ringworld series. Never expanded beyond its acronymic origin.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in the Ringworld series starting in 1970. One of the earliest examples of science fiction creating systematic replacement profanity.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderate all-purpose expletive. Its acronymic origin gives it an intellectual quality that distinguishes it from guttural fictional curses.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used throughout Known Space by various human cultures.
Example Quote: Tanj few people knew which end was which, these days.

Short Description: Thread is the deadly, worm-like rain from space that destroys all organic material. Invoking it is like cursing with the name of the thing you fear most.
Etymology (Narrative): Thread is a real, existential threat on Pern — parasitic organisms that fall from space and devour organic matter. Cursing with 'Thread' invokes genuine cosmic horror, like saying 'plague take you.'
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage through all Pern novels. Variants include 'threadspore' and 'thread warmed over.'
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout the Dragonriders of Pern series spanning multiple books from 1968 onward.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Deeply tied to worldbuilding. Thread is so feared that invoking it as profanity feels like tempting fate, giving the word genuine taboo weight.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal on Pern. All holds, weyrs, and crafthalls know and fear Thread.
Example Quote: Thread take you! I've had enough!

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Dren (Farscape); Crem (Stormlight); Druk (Star Wars); Dwang (Star Wars)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Dragonriders of Pern series by Anne McCaffrey
Short Description: Dragon droppings. Used as a general expletive among dragonriders who spend their days cleaning up after enormous dragons.
Etymology (Narrative): From the real English word 'fewmets' meaning deer droppings (a term from hunting). McCaffrey repurposed it for dragon excrement, and because dragonriders work with it daily, it naturally became their go-to scatological curse.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent usage throughout the series. A term of art that doubled as profanity.
Usage History (Narrative): Used across the Pern novel series. Notable because it's based on a real (if archaic) English word.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A realistic example of profession-specific profanity. The people who deal with the substance daily are the ones who curse with it.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Specific to dragonrider culture. Non-riders might not use it.
Example Quote: Oh, fewmets! The hatchling got into the stores again.

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Supergirl and the Legion of Super-Heroes #32 (2007)
Short Description: A 31st-century expletive used by the Legion of Super-Heroes. General-purpose expression of frustration.
Etymology (Narrative): Part of the Legion's future slang vocabulary. No specific etymology given — it's simply a word that evolved as profanity in the 31st century.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used consistently in Legion comics from various eras.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in various Legion of Super-Heroes comics. Part of the team's distinctive future vocabulary.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderate future-Earth expletive. Part of the comic's worldbuilding effort to show language evolution over a millennium.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): 31st-century Earth and United Planets.
Example Quote: Aw, grife! I will not drown in a roomful of wheat!

Short Description: The Legion of Super-Heroes' equivalent of the F-word. Used primarily as an adjective ('sprocking').
Etymology (Narrative): A 31st-century expletive. No in-universe etymology provided. Part of the Legion's future English dialect.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent as the strongest curse in the Legion vocabulary.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in various Legion of Super-Heroes publications from the 2000s onward.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): The strongest term in the Legion's profanity system. Functions exactly like its real-world equivalent.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): 31st-century Earth and United Planets.
Example Quote: Join the Legion, see the galaxy, get your sprocking brains beat out.

Short Description: The primary expletive in Marvel's 2099 future timeline. Used as a substitute for 'fuck' in all contexts.
Etymology (Narrative): In Marvel's 2099 future, language has evolved. 'Shock' — evoking electrical shock in a high-tech world — became the dominant expletive. Mirrors how technology shapes language.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent throughout the 2099 comic line. Used in all 2099 titles.
Usage History (Narrative): Introduced in Spider-Man 2099 (1992). Used across the entire Marvel 2099 publishing line.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Functions as the standard F-word replacement in a tech-heavy future. The technological association gives it a cyberpunk edge.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal in the 2099 timeline. All social strata use it.
Example Quote: What the shock is going on here?

Notable Speaker: Dinobot, Rattrap, various Maximals and Predacons
Short Description: The primary expletive in Beast Wars. Versatile — used as insult, exclamation, and modifier. In British English, 'slag' is already slang for 'slut.'
Etymology (Narrative): In the Transformers context, slag is industrial waste from metal smelting — fitting for a race of mechanical beings. For British audiences, the existing vulgar meaning of 'slag' added an extra layer.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used consistently throughout Beast Wars (1996-1999). In later Transformers media, 'scrap' became more common.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout Beast Wars' three seasons. In Transformers: Prime and other later series, 'scrap' and 'slag' are both used.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Moderate to strong depending on the audience. British viewers found it stronger due to its pre-existing vulgar meaning.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal among Cybertronians in the Beast Wars era.
Example Quote: What in the slag are you doing?

Short Description: General-purpose Cybertronian expletive. Like 'slag,' it derives from mechanical/industrial processes appropriate for a robot civilization.
Etymology (Narrative): Scrap metal is waste in metalworking. For Cybertronians, being compared to scrap metal is like being called worthless trash.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Became more prominent as 'slag' was phased out in some Transformers media. Now used alongside it.
Usage History (Narrative): Prominent in Transformers: Prime (2010-2013) and subsequent media.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderate expletive. Replaced 'slag' in some contexts, possibly due to British English concerns about the latter.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Universal among Cybertronians.
Example Quote: Oh, scrap! Decepticons!

First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Noughts and Crosses by Malorie Blackman (2001)
Short Description: In the inverted-racism world of Noughts and Crosses, 'blanker' is the slur used by the dominant black Crosses against the oppressed white Noughts.
Etymology (Narrative): Derived from 'blank' — implying whiteness is emptiness or nothingness. In the series' inverted racial hierarchy, it functions exactly as the N-word does in our world.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Used consistently in the novel series and BBC TV adaptation. Carries increasing weight as the political situation in the story escalates.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout Malorie Blackman's Noughts and Crosses series (2001-2019) and the BBC TV adaptation (2020).
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Designed to make predominantly white readers experience the impact of a racial slur directed at them, creating empathy through discomfort.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by the dominant Cross (black) population against the marginalized Nought (white) population.
Example Quote: Don't talk to that blanker. They're not worth your time.

Franchise: Space Cases / Star Trek: New Frontier
Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Dank Farrik (Star Wars); Gorram (Firefly); Shazbot (Mork & Mindy)
Notable Speaker: Catalina (Jewel Staite) in Space Cases; Captain Calhoun in Star Trek: New Frontier
Short Description: A curse from the planet Xenex, used by Captain Mackenzie Calhoun. Also used in Space Cases by the same creator.
Etymology (Narrative): Created by writer Peter David. Used in both Space Cases (a children's sci-fi show) and his Star Trek: New Frontier novel series, making it a cross-franchise fictional curse by the same author.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent across both franchises where Peter David used it.
Usage History (Narrative): First appeared in Space Cases (1996), then used extensively in Star Trek: New Frontier novels (1997 onward). A rare case of a fictional curse crossing franchises via the same writer.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Moderate expletive. Appropriate for a children's TV show while still carrying weight in adult novels.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Xenexian in origin (Star Trek). Also used on Catalina's homeworld (Space Cases).
Example Quote: Avoid celebrating the most exhausting holiday in the universe? Oh grozit, no!

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Frak (BSG); Grozit (Star Trek: New Frontier); PetaQ (Star Trek Klingon)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Star Trek: The Fall of Terok Nor (2000)
Short Description: A Ferengi exclamation used by Quark, expressing shock or dismay.
Etymology (Narrative): Part of the Ferengi language. Used by Quark in the novel continuity.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Limited to novel appearances. No evolution observed.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in Star Trek novels, primarily in Deep Space Nine tie-ins.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): A moderate Ferengi expletive.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Ferengi. Used by the profit-obsessed species.
Example Quote: Frinx! Quark gasped, as a strong hand grabbed him.

Related Words (Cross-Franchise): Skinjob (Blade Runner); S'wit (Elder Scrolls); Sleemo (Star Wars)
First Appearance (Episode/Chapter/Issue): Star Trek: The Next Generation (various episodes)
Short Description: The most iconic Klingon insult. Implies the target is without honor — the worst thing a Klingon can say about someone.
Etymology (Narrative): From the Klingon language (developed by linguist Marc Okrand). In Klingon warrior culture, honor is everything. petaQ strips the target of all honor and worth.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent throughout Star Trek media. Always carries extreme contempt from Klingon speakers.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout TNG, DS9, and other Star Trek series. One of the most recognized Klingon words among fans.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Carries the maximum taboo weight in Klingon culture. Being called petaQ is grounds for a duel to the death. It's their most emotionally devastating insult.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Klingon. Used across the Klingon Empire. Understood by many Federation citizens.
Example Quote: You are without honor! petaQ!

Short Description: A Goa'uld term meaning 'traitor,' specifically one who betrays the false gods. Applied to Teal'c after he defects to the SGC.
Etymology (Narrative): In the Goa'uld language. Since the Goa'uld present themselves as gods, betrayal is simultaneously treason and heresy. The word carries both political and religious weight.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Initially directed at Teal'c specifically. As more Jaffa rebel, the term becomes more broadly applied but loses none of its venom.
Usage History (Narrative): Used throughout Stargate SG-1's ten seasons. Primarily directed at Teal'c. Central to the show's themes of liberation from false gods.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Carries the maximum weight of both political and religious betrayal. In a theocratic society, there's nothing worse.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used throughout the Goa'uld Empire. All Jaffa understand it.
Example Quote: Shol'va! You will die for your betrayal!

Short Description: A mild exclamation used in the Reckoners series' future Earth. Generally considered one of Sanderson's less successful invented curses.
Etymology (Narrative): In a world where superpowered beings (Epics) have taken over, 'sparks' may relate to the energy associated with their powers. However, it's an existing English word without strong taboo associations.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Consistent but limited to the Reckoners trilogy.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in the Reckoners trilogy (2013-2016). Often cited in discussions of fictional profanity as an example of a less convincing invented curse.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Considered too mild by many readers. It's an actual word without inherent taboo, making it feel like a 1950s-style softened oath rather than genuine future profanity.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Future Earth. No regional specificity noted.
Example Quote: Sparks! That Epic nearly killed us!

Short Description: Borrowed from Mork & Mindy and incorporated into the Tribes video game series as a voice command emote, giving it a second life in gaming culture.
Etymology (Narrative): Originally from Mork & Mindy (1978). Dynamix/Sierra included it as a voice command in Starsiege: Tribes (1998), where it became one of the game's most spammed and beloved voice lines.
Semantic Drift Timeline: Evolved from a TV catchphrase to a gaming meme. In Tribes, it was used for everything from genuine frustration to comedic spam.
Usage History (Narrative): Used in Starsiege: Tribes (1998), Tribes 2 (2001), Tribes: Ascend (2012). The voice command system made it iconic in competitive gaming.
Taboo Trajectory (Narrative): Transitioned from pop culture to gaming culture, gaining new life and new meaning as a multi-purpose voice command emote.
Regional Notes (In-Universe): Used by all factions in the Tribes games.
Example Quote: VGTG VGS! (I am the greatest! Shazbot!)
